var-2025-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The Pedro II passage piles up his achievements — economic growth, flourishing arts, military victories, the abolition of slavery, attractive to immigrants — and then mentions the coup almost as a non sequitur. His ability as a ruler isn't the reason he was overthrown. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is IMPLIED about Pedro II?' — so you're hunting for a claim the passage SUPPORTS without stating it outright. Read the whole paragraph first and notice the strange gap between his good record and his removal; that gap is usually where the implication lives.

steps.title: Locate the achievements
steps.text: The passage stacks up positives: 'Brazil's economy grew, the arts scene flourished and the emperor steered his country to victory in a string of international disputes', plus 'the abolition of slavery (in 1888)' and Brazil 'becoming increasingly attractive to disgruntled Europeans'. That's a lot of evidence of competent rule.

steps.title: Locate the removal
steps.text: Pedro 'was unexpectedly overthrown' by a 'military coup' on 15 November 1889 — and he 'put up little resistance'. The passage frames this as 'an inglorious end' to an empire that had 'enjoyed new glory days under Pedro's care'. The removal isn't tied to any failure on his part.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Inglorious' = shameful, unworthy. 'Coup' = a sudden, often violent seizure of power. 'Disgruntled' = unhappy, dissatisfied. 'Forward-thinking' = progressive. These words paint Pedro as the progressive figure and the coup as the unworthy event.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says European immigration was 'highly unpopular' — the passage says the opposite, Brazil 'became increasingly attractive' to them. B says Brazil declined under him — the passage says it grew. C blames the exile on his treatment of his daughter — the passage simply notes Isabel wouldn't inherit; there's no causal link to the coup. D says his removal 'was not in any obvious way related to his ability as a ruler' — exactly the gap the passage builds.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The whole point of the paragraph is the disconnect between Pedro's strong record and his abrupt removal — that disconnect IS the implication.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume that a sudden wave of immigrants ('arrived there in waves') must have stirred up local resentment — that's a familiar historical pattern.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says Brazil 'became increasingly attractive' to Europeans — it doesn't mention any unpopularity at home. Step 5's option-by-option check catches that A imports a complaint the text never makes.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the word 'inglorious' and read it as a verdict on Pedro's reign rather than on the coup itself, then conclude things must have been getting worse.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 shows the opposite — economic growth, flourishing arts, victories, abolition. 'Inglorious' modifies 'end', not the reign that preceded it; the reign is described as 'new glory days'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to connect the two unusual details — the daughter being denied the throne, and the military coup — into one story.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage simply lists Isabel's exclusion as Pedro's only traditional move, then independently reports the coup. There's no causal language ('therefore', 'as a result', 'because of this') linking them. Step 3's frame keeps you honest about what the passage actually claims.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'what is implied' items where a passage stacks positives and then reports something bad happening, the implication is usually the GAP itself — the lack of an obvious cause. Scan for the option that names the absence ('not related to', 'unconnected with', 'despite his') rather than an active cause.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: When a passage mentions an unusual decision (here, disinheriting Isabel) right before reporting a major event (the coup), it's tempting to read causation into the adjacency. The cure: ask whether the passage uses causal language. If it just lists two facts side by side, treat them as independent until the text says otherwise.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The Chernobyl passage compares wildlife in the evacuated zone with four uncontaminated reserves and finds Chernobyl 'matched or exceeded' them — and Smith concludes that radiation effects are 'not as large as the negative effects of having people there'. The main point is that wildlife thrives where humans aren't. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: 'What is the main point?' — find the single idea the whole paragraph is built to support, not a side detail. With main-point items you read once, then ask: if I had to title this paragraph, what would I say?

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The closing quote from Smith carries the argument: 'Whatever negative effects there are from radiation, they are not as large as the negative effects of having people there.' That's the author's headline — radiation is bad, but people are worse.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: when humans left Chernobyl after the disaster, wildlife densities matched or beat what you see in clean reserves. So removing humans helped the animals MORE than radiation hurt them. The takeaway is about HUMAN ABSENCE, not about radiation tolerance specifically.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Fallout' = the radioactive particles spread by a nuclear accident. 'Resettled' = moved to a new place to live. 'Abundance' = how many of something. 'Density' = how many per unit area. These let you track the comparison: Chernobyl's density vs. clean reserves' density.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A talks about humans returning to the area — the passage doesn't discuss when (or whether) humans can come back. B says wildlife 'recovered since the disaster' — true-ish, but it misses the comparative point about clean reserves. C says wildlife is less affected by radiation than humans are — the passage compares radiation effects to human-presence effects, not radiation effects on different species. D says a human-free environment is highly beneficial for wildlife — exactly what Smith concludes.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The whole study Smith ran is set up to compare a human-free contaminated zone with human-free clean zones — and the finding is that the absence of humans wins out. That's the headline.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a Chernobyl passage is to think the lesson is about radioactive risk — 'this place is still dangerous for people'. That's the headline outside this paragraph, in news coverage generally.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage never discusses human return at all. Step 3's paraphrase shows the whole frame is animals-in-the-zone, not humans-back-to-the-zone.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'density in Chernobyl matched or exceeded those in other reserves' and read 'recovery' — animals are doing well, that's the point.
distractors.why_wrong: Recovery is part of the story but isn't the takeaway. The author's framing — via Smith's quote in step 2 — is the COMPARISON between radiation-harm and human-presence-harm. B misses the comparative insight that makes the paragraph interesting.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading of 'negative effects are not as large' as 'animals are less affected than humans' is a tempting shortcut — both are about radiation tolerance.
distractors.why_wrong: Smith is comparing two CAUSES (radiation vs. human presence) within wildlife, not comparing wildlife with humans. Step 5's option-by-option check catches the swap.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'main point' items where a passage ends in a direct quote from an expert, that quote is almost always the headline. Read it once, paraphrase it in one sentence, and match THAT sentence to the options — don't get sidetracked by the build-up.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: On nuclear/disaster topics, prior-knowledge bias pulls you toward stock claims ('the area is still dangerous', 'wildlife recovered') even when the passage is making a different point. The cure: ignore what you 'know' about Chernobyl from other sources and stay inside this paragraph.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The introductory paragraphs say Nightingale 'established the principles of modern nursing and hospital sanitation' 200 years ago and that today's nurses follow her playbook around the world. Her revolutionary ideas have become the global standard. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about Florence Nightingale and nursing in the INTRODUCTORY paragraphs. So you stay in the opening section — the first two or three paragraphs — and look for a claim the passage supports by inference, not by direct statement.

steps.title: Locate the framing
steps.text: Two anchors carry the meaning. First: Nightingale 'established the principles of modern nursing and hospital sanitation'. Second: 'If she were to drop in on a hospital today, Nightingale would be pleased to see the progress in nursing since her day'. So the principles she founded are now ordinary practice — pleased = recognises her own ideas in action.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Two hundred years ago, Nightingale's ideas — hygiene, evidence, statistical infographics — were revolutionary; today, they're just how nursing is done everywhere. The intro is celebrating how thoroughly her once-radical principles became standard.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Pioneering' = breaking new ground, doing something first. 'Infographic' = a chart that shows information visually. 'Sanitation' = keeping things clean to prevent disease. 'Established the principles' = laid down the rules others now follow.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says she was guided by intuition rather than facts — the passage emphasises her statistical work (pie chart, Royal Statistical Society), so this is backwards. B says her revolutionary ideas have long been common practice — that's the 'pleased to see the progress' frame exactly. C says she endured male prejudice over her statistics — the passage notes she WAS the first woman in the Royal Statistical Society but doesn't mention prejudice. D says her reputation rests on practical work over scientific insights — the passage gives BOTH equal weight and praises her statistical insight specifically.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The intro's whole 'Nightingale would be pleased' framing depends on the idea that her revolutionary principles have become routine — that's the implication.

distractors.why_tempting: Many associate 19th-century reform with passion-and-intuition rather than data — and you might miss the pie chart detail on a first read.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 highlights her admission to the Royal Statistical Society 'for her pioneering work in statistical infographics' — explicitly a fact-based reformer, not an intuitive one. A is the opposite of what the passage says.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line about her being the FIRST WOMAN in the Royal Statistical Society, it's natural to fill in the surrounding context with the prejudice she must have faced as a 19th-century pioneer.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage MENTIONS the achievement but doesn't describe any prejudice she endured. C imports a plausible but unstated detail. Step 5's option check requires what the passage actually says, not what we can guess about Victorian-era sexism.

distractors.why_tempting: Vivid practical images — tending soldiers in Crimea — pull your eye to the hands-on side of her work, making you weight it more than the statistical side.
distractors.why_wrong: The intro praises BOTH her practical reforms AND her scientific insights (the pie chart used to persuade officials). Step 3's paraphrase treats them as inseparable. D forces a hierarchy the passage rejects.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'what is implied' items in an introduction, look for the COUNTERFACTUAL — the 'if she could see today' move that authors use to mark how much has changed. The contrast between 'her day' and 'now' is usually where the implication lives.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Biographical passages about reformers tempt you to add common knowledge (sexism, hardship, intuition) that the text never asserts. The cure: hold every option against an explicit anchor in the passage; if you can't underline a supporting line, drop the option.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The middle paragraphs describe nurses now performing anaesthesia, abdominal/orthopaedic/cardiac surgery, emergency caesareans and chronic-care management — all 'tasks conventionally reserved for doctors'. The line between nurse and doctor is blurring. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is ARGUED about PRESENT-DAY nursing. So you look at the sections about today and the near future, and find the claim the author is actively making — not just reporting.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. First: 'nurses will be doing a growing number of tasks conventionally reserved for doctors'. Second: the specific examples — two-thirds of US anaesthetics from nurse anaesthetists, UK nurses doing 'abdominal, orthopaedic and cardiac surgery', sub-Saharan nurses doing 'emergency caesarean sections'. The pattern: nurses moving into doctor territory.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Modern nurses aren't just bedside helpers anymore — they're doing the kinds of procedures we used to think only doctors did. The old wall between the two professions is coming down. That's the author's argument.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Conventionally reserved' = traditionally only allowed to one group. 'Anaesthetics' = drugs that put patients to sleep for surgery. 'Caesarean section' = a surgical delivery of a baby. 'Holistic' = considering the whole person, not just the symptoms. 'Confidant' = someone you trust with private information.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says modern nursing is still 'mainly focused on taking care of patients' practical needs' — the passage says the opposite: nurses are taking on doctor-level technical work. B says safety depends on collaboration — the passage doesn't frame nurse-doctor relations as collaboration; it frames them as nurses ABSORBING doctor functions. C warns of a risk of losing personal qualities — the passage celebrates nurses as confidants and emphasises empathy will REMAIN. D says nursing is blurring the doctor-nurse distinction — exactly the headline from step 2.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The whole middle of the article is one example after another of nurses doing what doctors used to do — that ROLE EXPANSION is what's being argued.

distractors.why_tempting: If you imagine 'nursing' as the bedside-care image from older media, this option seems natural — and 'practical needs' sounds humble and accurate.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2's evidence — anaesthesia, major surgery, emergency procedures — directly contradicts 'mainly practical care'. A describes nursing as the author claims it NO LONGER is.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Patient safety through nurse-doctor collaboration' sounds like a reasonable healthcare claim — most people would agree with it as a general statement.
distractors.why_wrong: The article doesn't frame the relationship as collaborative; it frames it as nurses TAKING OVER doctor tasks. B describes a different (also reasonable) model the article isn't making. Step 5's match check catches this.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading of 'machines will not replace human beings' as a warning about losing the human touch is plausible — many tech-and-care articles do worry about that.
distractors.why_wrong: The article presents the opposite — empathy stays even as nurses gain technical roles ('healing powers remain rooted in empathy'). C inverts the article's tone of confidence into anxiety.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-034.technique: On 'what is argued in connection with X' items, scan for the pattern of REPEATED EXAMPLES — when an author lists three or four cases in a row (US anaesthesia + UK surgery + African caesarean), they're building toward one general claim. Pick the option that names that general claim.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: Multi-example paragraphs let you pick any one example as a 'point' — but the author's argument is the PATTERN across all of them, not any single case. The cure: ask what all the examples have in common; that common feature is the argument.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The passage explicitly says 'the transformation of nursing has been uneven' and gives India, Germany and Portugal as places where nurses are 'still largely treated as doctors' minions'. Changes have left traces only in some parts of the world. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: 'Which of the following statements is most in accordance with the text?' — find the option that matches a specific claim the passage actually makes (not just one that sounds plausible). This is a 'find the supported statement' item; you need an option you can underline in the text.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The later section directly states: 'the transformation of nursing has been uneven. In countries as varied as India, Germany and Portugal, nurses are still largely treated as doctors' minions and may not even diagnose common ailments or prescribe medication.' That's a black-and-white statement that the changes are geographically patchy.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The modernisation of nursing has only reached some countries. Even in rich countries like Germany and Portugal, nurses haven't gained the doctor-level autonomy seen elsewhere. So the global picture is patchy, not uniform.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Uneven' = not consistent, varies from place to place. 'Minions' = subordinates with little power. 'Diagnose' = identify what illness someone has. 'Prescribe' = order medication. The combo paints a picture of nurses with low autonomy — the OPPOSITE of the empowered nurses described earlier.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says patient connections have suffered from recent developments — the passage emphasises nurses REMAIN trusted confidants ('nurses invariably come top' in trust surveys). B says recent changes left traces in only some parts of the world — exactly the 'uneven transformation' sentence from step 2. C says the new nurse role is a 'global success story' — the passage explicitly rejects this with the India/Germany/Portugal counterexample. D says modern nursing brought new political insights — the passage actually complains nurses are 'often not at the table' in health policy.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The text's word 'uneven' and its concrete list of laggard countries are exactly what B paraphrases. The other options reverse or invent claims the article doesn't make.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read a story about nurses taking on technical work and worry that the personal side must be suffering — that's a common tech-vs.-humanity narrative.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's surveys say nurses 'invariably come top' for trust, and the closing line stresses that 'healing powers remain rooted in empathy'. A invents a tension the text actively denies. Step 5 catches this.

distractors.why_tempting: The list of impressive new roles (anaesthesia, surgery, caesareans) makes 'global success story' feel right — those are big wins.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'uneven transformation' sentence in step 2 is the exact opposite — the new role hasn't reached India, Germany or Portugal yet. C cherry-picks the wins and ignores the explicit qualifier.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'health-policy decisions' in the passage and 'modern nursing' is the headline topic, D might feel like a natural inference about how the profession is influencing policy.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says nurses are 'often not at the table when health-policy decisions are made' — they are EXCLUDED, not contributing insights. Step 5's matching catches the inversion.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On 'most in accordance with the text' items, scan for an option whose KEY WORD ('uneven', 'patchy', 'global', 'always') appears in or directly paraphrases a sentence in the passage. If you can't underline a supporting line, the option is too generic.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: Long passages with both positive and negative claims tempt you to pick whichever side feels more recent in your memory. The cure: re-locate the specific qualifier ('uneven', 'still', 'however') — those words flag the author's actual stance and overrule the surrounding cheerleading.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The passage warns the world will be 'short of 7.6m nurses' by 2030 — 'a third of their number today' — and adds 'no profession has a higher number of vacancies' in many countries. From a job-seeker's angle, that's plenty of positions waiting to be filled. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about JOB OPPORTUNITIES for nurses. So you read the passage's claims about labour-market conditions, then flip them to the job-seeker's perspective — a shortage of nurses means plenty of jobs available.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Three anchors stack the case. 'In many countries, no profession has a higher number of vacancies.' 'In the next decade, the shortage of nurses will remain the biggest problem that national health systems all over the world will face.' 'By 2030, the world will be short of 7.6m nurses, which is a third of their number today.' All three describe a massive unfilled-positions problem.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Hospitals everywhere need way more nurses than they have. The shortage is huge — by 2030 it'll grow to roughly a third of the global workforce. For someone thinking 'should I become a nurse?', the implication is: yes, the jobs will be there.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Vacancies' = unfilled job positions. 'Lost its lustre' = become less attractive or prestigious. 'Hard to fill' = employers can't find candidates. 'Shortage' = not enough supply. These are all employer-side complaints, but from the job-seeker side they translate to opportunity.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says there will be plenty of positions in most parts of the world — that's exactly the shortage-flipped-to-opportunity reading. B says old-style nursing will be done by robots — the passage actually says the opposite, 'nursing may be the only aspect... in which machines will not replace human beings'. C says the prospects look dim — the passage paints prospects as wide open (lots of jobs, technology making work easier). D says today's lack of nurses will change in years ahead — the passage explicitly says it will REMAIN the biggest problem and grow worse.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. A massive nurse shortage means lots of open positions — that's what 'job opportunities' looks like from the candidate side. The passage stacks three explicit anchors for this.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'technology will be roped in' and 'machines' from the closing paragraph, you might jump to robots replacing routine nursing work — that's a familiar 'automation' narrative.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says nursing 'may be the only aspect of the health-care profession in which machines will not replace human beings'. B inverts the article's specific exception. Step 5 catches this directly.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'nursing has lost its lustre' and read the whole story as bleak — if the field is unattractive, prospects must be dim.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Lost its lustre' is about RECRUITMENT (people don't want to enter the field) — not about job availability. From a job-seeker's view, unattractive-to-others means even more competition-free openings. Step 3's plain-English flip catches this.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a passage about reform and modernisation is to assume current problems are being solved — surely the shortage will improve.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's projection is the opposite: by 2030 the shortage GROWS to 7.6 million. The article describes efforts to attract more nurses, but it doesn't predict success. D treats hope as fact.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'what is implied about job opportunities' items, look for the workforce-shortage statistic. A staffing shortage on the employer side = job availability on the candidate side. Always do that perspective flip — passages describe the problem from the employer angle, options often describe it from the candidate angle.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: Passages about a profession 'in trouble' (low prestige, high vacancies, hard to fill) sound negative, which tempts you toward 'prospects are dim'. But the trouble IS the opportunity — nobody else wants the jobs, so they're open. Read each negative as its positive flip side before matching.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The closing paragraphs say 'Technology will be roped in to make their work more manageable and reduce burnout' and that machines will NOT replace nurses themselves. Technology helps nurses — it doesn't replace them. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is said about the FUTURE OF NURSING AND TECHNOLOGY. Find the part of the passage that pairs those two topics and identify the relationship the author claims — supportive, threatening, neutral, or mixed.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two lines do the work. 'Technology will be roped in to make their work more manageable and reduce burnout.' And: 'as diagnostic systems and surgical robots advance, nursing may be the only aspect of the health-care profession in which machines will not replace human beings.' Technology helps nurses; it doesn't displace them.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Better tech will make nursing easier and prevent burnout. And while other parts of healthcare will see machines take over jobs, nursing won't — the human element is too central. So technology is an ally, not a threat.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Roped in' = brought in to help. 'Burnout' = exhaustion from overwork. 'Diagnostic systems' = machines that figure out what's wrong with a patient. 'Surgical robots' = machines that perform surgery. The author lists the threats, then carves out nursing as the human exception.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says there will be a conflict between human and technological aspects — the passage describes harmony, not conflict; tech helps. B says nursing will change 'beyond recognition' — the passage says core nursing values STAY rooted in empathy; recognition is preserved. C says nursing will in various ways benefit from modern developments — exactly the 'roped in to make work manageable' frame. D says innovation will result in inferior care — the article predicts the opposite.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The closing paragraphs are unambiguously positive about the tech-nurse relationship: tech makes the work easier without erasing the human core. C captures both halves cleanly.

distractors.why_tempting: Tech-vs.-humanity is a stock framing, and if you remember 'machines will not replace human beings' you might read it as evidence of a tension.
distractors.why_wrong: That line isn't a warning about conflict — it's a reassurance that the two coexist. Step 2's full quote is supportive, not adversarial.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the cascade of changes throughout the passage (new roles, university degrees, surgical specialisations), 'beyond recognition' might feel like a reasonable summary of just how different modern nursing is.
distractors.why_wrong: The closing line stresses 'its healing powers remain rooted in empathy and a human touch' — explicitly the part that DOESN'T change. The continuity is part of the author's point. Step 3 keeps you from overshooting into 'unrecognisable'.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading of 'machines won't replace nurses' could go bleak — maybe the machines will be inadequate substitutes elsewhere and care will suffer.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't claim that. It treats tech as a positive force (reduces burnout, manageable work) and treats nurses as the irreplaceable human centre. D imports a worry the article doesn't voice.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'what is said about X and technology' items, find the verb the author chooses for the relationship ('roped in', 'replace', 'displace', 'support', 'augment'). The verb fixes the polarity: 'roped in' and 'support' are positive; 'replace' and 'displace' are negative. Match the option to the verb's polarity.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: On technology articles, you can pick the most dramatic option ('beyond recognition', 'inferior care', 'conflict') because drama sounds analytical. But the author here is measured, even reassuring. Read the actual modifiers — 'manageable', 'reduce burnout', 'rooted in empathy' — and let them set the tone.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The Bread passage says first farming communities had a diet that was 'less nutritionally diverse than that of earlier hunter gatherers' — bread fed more people but didn't feed them better. The implication is that adopting bread didn't generally improve nutritional health. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED in connection with the FIRST FARMING COMMUNITIES — so you stay in the Bread paragraph, find the claim about diet/nutrition, and pick the option that follows from it by inference.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The crucial line: 'Although the agriculturalist diet was less nutritionally diverse than that of earlier hunter gatherers, the importance of bread in the development of human society shouldn't be underestimated.' Diversity went DOWN with farming, even though society advanced.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Farming let civilisations grow because it produced lots of cereal — but the actual food on the plate was less varied than what hunter-gatherers ate. Bread fed more mouths but didn't necessarily feed them better. So nutrition didn't improve when farming arrived; if anything, it got worse.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Nutritionally diverse' = containing a wide variety of nutrients. 'Hunter gatherers' = people who get food by hunting animals and gathering wild plants. 'Cereal' = grain crops like wheat and barley. 'Non-procurement activities' = activities that aren't about getting food (like art and literature).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says early farmers ate more healthily — the passage says the opposite ('less nutritionally diverse'). B says bread's importance has been exaggerated — the passage says importance 'shouldn't be underestimated', i.e. the opposite. C says bread's introduction did not generally lead to a more healthy food intake — that's exactly the 'less nutritionally diverse' line in flipped phrasing. D says bread's role is hotly debated — the passage presents the role as established, not debated.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The 'less nutritionally diverse' clause is the explicit anchor — and 'did not lead to a more healthy food intake' is the same idea in plainer English.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume the agricultural revolution made everything better — that's the standard 'progress' narrative most readers carry into the text.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage directly inverts that narrative: diet diversity DROPPED with farming. Step 2's anchor is unambiguous, and A says the opposite.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a passage that hedges ('shouldn't be underestimated') is to think the author is qualifying a claim — maybe even pushing back against an inflated reputation.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Shouldn't be underestimated' = the importance is real, not exaggerated. The author is DEFENDING bread's role, not deflating it. Step 5 catches the inversion.

distractors.why_tempting: Academic-sounding paragraphs about ancient history might suggest scholarly debate is going on in the background — that's a familiar register.
distractors.why_wrong: There's no debate in the text — the author makes one claim and the claim isn't contested. D imports a controversy the passage doesn't stage.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'what is implied' items where a passage contains a concession clause ('although X, Y'), the X-half is usually the implication and the Y-half is the surface claim. Here 'Although the diet was less nutritionally diverse' is the implication; 'importance shouldn't be underestimated' is the surface point. Pick the option that paraphrases the X-half.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: Although-clauses sound minor — they're grammatically subordinate — so we read past them. But on HP they're often the EXACT implication the question wants. The cure: read every 'although' / 'while' / 'despite' clause TWICE on first pass.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The African Art paragraph says the Museum of Black Civilizations is 'designed to be a creative laboratory that will help shape the continent's future sense of identity'. Its purpose is broad African self-definition, not just art display. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what we are TOLD about the Museum of Black Civilizations — so you stay inside the African Art paragraph and pick the option backed by an explicit line in the text.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The mission statement is the anchor: the museum is 'designed to be a creative laboratory that will help shape the continent's future sense of identity'. The second anchor is the restitution mission: 'reclaim some of the continent's lost past', with Senegal demanding the return of stolen artworks. Both anchors point to broad pan-African consciousness.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The museum isn't just a building for African art — it's an institution meant to help define what 'African' identity means going forward. It's about cultural self-determination across the continent.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Repository' = a place where things are kept. 'Creative laboratory' = a space for experimenting with ideas. 'Restitution' = giving back something taken (here, stolen art). 'Pan-African' (not in the text but useful) = covering the whole continent of Africa.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says one chief aim is to promote African consciousness in a wide sense — that's exactly the 'creative laboratory shaping the continent's future sense of identity' line. B says most exhibits derive from European museums — the passage says some pieces are LENT for the opening but doesn't say the majority do; also, 'many of the galleries are not yet filled'. C says the main purpose is reminding Africa of its colonial past — restitution is one part, but the central mission per step 2 is forward-looking (future identity), not backward-looking. D says space is reserved for European artworks — the passage doesn't say this.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The 'creative laboratory for shaping future identity' framing is unambiguous, and 'African consciousness in a wide sense' captures it cleanly. The other options either invert (B) or reduce the museum's mission to one of its parts (C, D).

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'France, to lend pieces for the opening' it's easy to extrapolate that lots of European loans must be filling the galleries.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says galleries are 'not yet filled' and mentions loans only as an opening gesture — not that the majority of exhibits come from Europe. Step 5 keeps you to what the text actually says about the inventory.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'reclaim some of the continent's lost past' and read the museum as a colonial-redress project — which it partly is.
distractors.why_wrong: The MAIN purpose described is forward-looking — shaping FUTURE identity. The colonial-redress is a secondary plot. C promotes a subordinate point to chief purpose. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the priority right.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap inference from the opening line ('most prominent homes for African art were within museums in Europe') — it's natural to assume the new museum carves out a European section in response.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes the museum as 'a repository for African art, culture and history' — there's no reserved European section. D invents a structural detail.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'what are we told about X' items where X is an institution with a mission statement, look for the future-oriented purpose verb ('shape', 'foster', 'promote', 'develop'). That verb is the institution's chief aim; the option that mirrors it wins.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: Institutions in HP passages usually have BOTH a backward-looking mission (restitution, preservation) AND a forward-looking one (shaping future identity, education). Distractors often promote the backward-looking part to chief purpose. The cure: if the text says BOTH, the chief purpose is usually the forward-looking one.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The John Falstaff paragraph describes him as 'fat, vain, cowardly... contemptuous of honourable virtues' and as introducing Prince Hal to a 'hedonistic lifestyle' — a man who scorns honour and lives for pleasure. Among the options, 'Immoral' captures that cluster best. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the ONE WORD that best describes Falstaff as Shakespeare depicts him — so you collect the adjectives the passage uses for him and pick the option that best summarises them.

steps.title: Locate the descriptions
steps.text: The character sketch lists: 'fat, vain, cowardly knight who is contemptuous of honourable virtues'. He also acts as a 'father ruffian' to Prince Hal and introduces him to a 'hedonistic lifestyle amongst the commoners'. Five adjectives plus two role descriptors — all in the same moral direction.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Falstaff is a knight without honour: fat, vain, scared of fights, openly dismissive of virtue, and a corrupting influence on the prince who would become Henry V. He's the picture of someone with no moral compass.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Contemptuous' = scornful, openly disrespectful. 'Hedonistic' = devoted to pleasure. 'Ruffian' = a rough, lawless person. 'Honourable virtues' = principles like courage, loyalty, integrity. Falstaff is contemptuous OF these — meaning he openly rejects them. That's a textbook description of immorality.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'Immoral' — captures contempt for virtue + corruption of the prince + hedonism. B 'Sympathetic' — the passage notes he's a 'comic character', not that he's sympathetic; sympathetic usually means we feel for someone, which the passage doesn't claim. C 'Disloyal' — there's no mention of him betraying anyone; cowardice and vice aren't disloyalty. D 'Generous' — nothing in the description suggests generosity; vanity is the opposite.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. Every adjective in the character sketch — vain, cowardly, contemptuous of virtue, hedonistic, corrupting — clusters around immorality. The other options each match one detail at best (B touches comedy, C and D are absent).

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember Falstaff from popular culture as a lovable comic figure, 'sympathetic' fits the affectionate way the passage opens ('one of the most famous comic characters').
distractors.why_wrong: 'Famous comic character' describes his fame and his role in comedy — not the audience's feelings toward him. The actual character traits listed are all moral negatives. Step 5 keeps you to the text, not to outside affection for the figure.

distractors.why_tempting: Cowardice and 'father ruffian' both lean toward someone untrustworthy — and untrustworthy slides easily into 'disloyal'.
distractors.why_wrong: Disloyalty implies betrayal of a specific person or cause; the passage doesn't say Falstaff betrays anyone. He's amoral and hedonistic, which is broader and different. The cluster of traits points to A, not C.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap guess: Falstaff entertains Prince Hal lavishly, so maybe he's generous with his time, money, friendship.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly calls him VAIN — caring about himself — which is the opposite of generous. There's no textual support for generosity. Step 5's option-by-option match catches this.

var-2025-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On 'one word best describes X' items, COLLECT the adjectives the passage uses for X and look for the option that subsumes the largest cluster of them. The right option usually covers 3+ of the listed traits; wrong options each match one trait at best.
var-2025-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: Famous characters tempt you to use prior cultural knowledge ('Falstaff is lovable', 'Henry V is heroic') instead of the passage's specific framing. The cure: pretend you've never heard the name and judge only from the adjectives in the paragraph.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: Gap 31 sits in 'Is it just a bug in our ethical _____?' Right before, the sentence talks about people sticking to moral rules even when breaking them gives better outcomes — the author is asking whether this stubbornness is a glitch in our moral PROCESSING (our mental machinery). 'Processing' is the metaphor 'bug' is borrowed from. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: This is a gap-fill (cloze) noun question — pick the word that best fits 'a bug in our ethical _____'. The trick is to use the WIDER context: 'bug' is computer-jargon for an error, so what's the thing that has bugs? Code. Software. Processing. The noun has to be a system or mechanism, not an emotion or judgement.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Just before the gap: 'Most people strictly adhere to moral rules... even when breaking them leads to a better outcome'. The whole paragraph is framing morality as a kind of mental SYSTEM that produces decisions — and asking whether the system has an error. So the missing word names the system itself.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The metaphor is consistent: 'bug' → 'processing'. A bug is a flaw in a process. The author is asking: is our moral PROCESSING buggy? That's the natural metaphorical fit. The four options each name something the bug could live inside; only one is a system/mechanism.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Confusion' = a state of being puzzled. 'Wrongdoing' = doing bad things. 'Condition' = a state or circumstance. 'Processing' = the act of working through information mentally (here, ethical decision-making as a mental process). Of these, only 'processing' names a SYSTEM that can have a 'bug'.

steps.title: Plug each option in and test
steps.text: A 'a bug in our ethical confusion' — confusion isn't a system; you don't have bugs in confusion. B 'a bug in our ethical wrongdoing' — wrongdoing is an act, not a system; also you'd want a flaw in the SYSTEM that PRODUCES wrongdoing. C 'a bug in our ethical condition' — condition is a state, not a process; the metaphor doesn't land. D 'a bug in our ethical processing' — processing is exactly the kind of cognitive operation that CAN have bugs. Only D preserves the metaphor.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Bug' fixes the register: we're talking about the SYSTEM that processes ethical decisions, and 'processing' is the only option that names a system. The other three name states, acts, or feelings — and you can't have a 'bug' in those.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to scan the moral-dilemma context and think the missing word is about mental disarray — confusion fits the puzzlement the author describes.
distractors.why_wrong: The collocation 'a bug in our ethical X' demands a system or mechanism for X. Confusion isn't a system; it's a state. Step 3's metaphor check catches the misfit.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the word 'ethical' and look for a moral noun — wrongdoing is the most moral-sounding option of the four.
distractors.why_wrong: 'A bug in our wrongdoing' reads as a flaw in the act of doing wrong, which doesn't make sense — the paragraph is about whether STICKING TO RULES is a glitch, not about glitches in wrongdoing. Step 5's plug-and-test shows the meaning collapses.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to read 'condition' as 'state of being' — and an ethical condition could metaphorically have a problem.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Condition' is too static for the 'bug' metaphor. Bugs live in operations, not states. The author keeps the computer metaphor consistent through 'processing' and 'maximising results' — condition breaks that consistency.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On cloze nouns paired with a metaphor word like 'bug', 'glitch', 'crash', 'rewire', READ THE METAPHOR FIRST and pick the noun that belongs to the metaphor's domain. 'Bug' → software/processing/code; 'crash' → systems; 'rewire' → wiring/circuits. The metaphor narrows four options to one.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: Cloze nouns let you match by semantic field ('ethical' → 'wrongdoing') instead of by metaphor coherence. The cure: identify the metaphor word in the sentence and let it set the domain. Here, 'bug' demands a system, not a moral noun.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: Gap 32 sits in 'A recent paper measures people's _____ behavior toward those who make such utilitarian decisions.' The paper measures REAL behaviour observed in experiments (not stated opinions) — 'actual' fits, marking the contrast between what people SAY and what they DO. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: This is a cloze adjective — pick the modifier that fits 'people's _____ behavior'. The trick is to use the surrounding paragraph: what's the AUTHOR'S contrast? The 2013 study (earlier in the paragraph) measured what people SAID. The 'recent paper' measures something different. Find the contrast and the adjective fixes itself.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Earlier: 'a 2013 study revealed that even when people CLAIM it is moral to... they view someone who does such a thing as lacking empathy and integrity.' The new paper goes beyond claims and measures observed behaviour in lab experiments. So the gap signals the shift from stated opinion to real conduct.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship the gap marks is STATED-VS-OBSERVED. 'Actual behaviour' is the natural pair to 'claimed opinion'. The adjective has to flag 'this is what people really do in experiments', not what they say. That fixes 'actual'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Past' = previous, earlier in time. 'Actual' = real, as opposed to claimed or imagined. 'Immoral' = ethically wrong. 'Current' = present-day, ongoing. 'Actual' is the precise English word that marks the contrast between 'what people say' and 'what people do' — the exact distinction the paragraph is set up to draw.

steps.title: Plug each option in and test
steps.text: A 'past behavior' — the paper measures present experimental behaviour, not historical behaviour; doesn't fit. B 'actual behavior' — exactly the stated-vs-observed contrast the paragraph builds. C 'immoral behavior' — the paragraph isn't claiming the subjects' behaviour is immoral; the dilemma is about how subjects RESPOND to others' utilitarian choices. D 'current behavior' — semantically thin; the paper isn't measuring 'current' behaviour as opposed to past, it's measuring real behaviour as opposed to claimed behaviour.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Actual' is the precise adjective that contrasts with 'claimed' — and the 'claim' verb appears explicitly in the preceding sentence. The cloze marks the experimental upgrade from self-report to observed behaviour.

distractors.why_tempting: If you see 'a 2013 study' just before the gap, 'past' might feel like a contrast — the recent paper looks at past behaviour somehow.
distractors.why_wrong: The recent paper measures behaviour in NEW experiments, not historical behaviour. 'Past' would reverse the time direction. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the misfit.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a moral-philosophy paragraph is to reach for moral adjectives — 'immoral behaviour' has the right ethical register.
distractors.why_wrong: The paper measures the JUDGE'S response, not the actor's morality. Subjects aren't accused of immoral behaviour; they're observed making trust judgements. C imports a moral framing the sentence doesn't carry.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading of 'a recent paper measures... behavior' might suggest 'current behaviour' as the natural collocation — current = recent.
distractors.why_wrong: Current is redundant with 'recent paper measures' — and it doesn't capture the stated-vs-observed contrast the author is drawing. The sentence wants a contrast adjective, not a temporal one. Step 3 isolates the actual relationship.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On cloze adjectives that come right after a study or paper is introduced, look for the CONTRAST the new study makes with what came before. If the previous sentence had 'claim' or 'say' or 'report', the gap usually wants 'actual' or 'real' or 'observed' — the adjective that marks the upgrade from talk to evidence.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: Cloze adjectives tempt you to pick the option that shares the topic's register ('moral paragraph' → 'immoral'). The cure: identify the contrast the SENTENCE makes (not the topic), and pick the adjective that names that contrast.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: Gap 33 sits in 'When those respondents said they would push a man off a footbridge... _____ rated them as less moral and trustworthy'. The sentence describes the OTHER PEOPLE in the experiment doing the rating — the participants. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: Cloze noun — pick the word that names the people doing the rating in the experiment. The earlier sentence describes the setup: psychologists asked 'American adults to respond to moral dilemmas and then interact with other supposed respondents online'. So the people doing the rating are study PARTICIPANTS, not the researchers.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Two sentences earlier: 'psychologists Jim Everett, Molly Crockett and David Pizarro asked American adults to respond to moral dilemmas and then interact with other supposed respondents online.' The American adults are the subjects — and they're the ones rating the (fictional) respondents who pushed the man. So the noun describes those rating subjects.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The gap fills a role label in an experiment. There are usually three groups in psychology studies: EXPERIMENTERS (the researchers), PARTICIPANTS (the subjects who take the test), and sometimes CONFEDERATES (fake subjects). The text already named the experimenters by name (Everett, Crockett, Pizarro). The remaining group doing the rating is the participants.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Applicants' = people applying for something (a job, a programme). 'Experimenters' = the researchers running a study. 'Interviewers' = people asking questions in an interview. 'Participants' = people taking part in a study or experiment. The standard psychology-research term for the rated-and-rating subjects is 'participants'.

steps.title: Plug each option in and test
steps.text: A 'applicants rated them' — there's no application process in this study; applicants doesn't fit the setting. B 'experimenters rated them' — the experimenters are Everett, Crockett, Pizarro, who designed the study; the rating is done by the subjects, not by the researchers. C 'interviewers rated them' — no interview format is described; the interaction is online over a moral dilemma. D 'participants rated them' — exactly the term for the American adults who took the experiment and rated their fake counterparts.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The paragraph describes an experiment with American adults as subjects; those subjects rate the fictional respondents. 'Participants' is the precise label for them. The other options each name a role that doesn't appear in this study.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read fast, 'applicants' has the right shape — people in some sort of formal process — and you might fill it in by sound.
distractors.why_wrong: There's no application to anything in the study; the subjects respond to dilemmas, they don't apply. Step 3's role check shows 'applicant' isn't a role in this experiment.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'psychologists' two sentences back and think the experimenters did the rating — that's a plausible reading of who would judge moral choices.
distractors.why_wrong: The experimenters are named (Everett, Crockett, Pizarro) and they DESIGNED the study; the rating was done by the subjects to test their trust responses. The whole point of the experiment is what SUBJECTS think of utilitarian peers.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a study about asking people questions: 'interviewers' has the right professional ring.
distractors.why_wrong: The format described is not an interview — subjects answered dilemmas and interacted online with fake peers. There are no interviewers in the setup. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the mismatch.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On cloze nouns that name an actor in an experiment, list the THREE STANDARD ROLES — experimenters, participants, confederates — and pick the one the sentence requires. The named individuals (Everett, Crockett, Pizarro) are usually the experimenters; the rated subjects are usually the participants.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: Cloze role-nouns tempt you to pick by sound rather than by who actually does the action in the sentence. The cure: ask 'who is doing the verb here?' (rating, in this case) and locate that actor's role label.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: Gap 34 sits in 'we want our friends to at least flinch before personally _____ others.' The whole paragraph is about pushing a man off a footbridge — the utilitarian choice harms someone to save others. 'Harming' fits the deliberate-injury context perfectly. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: Cloze gerund (verb-ing) — pick the verb that fits 'flinch before personally _____ others'. The clue is 'flinch': we want a moment of moral hesitation before doing something to another person. The verb has to name what causes hesitation — usually an action with bad consequences.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Earlier in the paragraph: 'push a man off a footbridge to block a trolley from killing five rail workers'. The moral dilemma is whether to DELIBERATELY HURT one person to save others. The sentence with the gap generalises this: friends should hesitate before personally [doing what utilitarianism asks them to do]. That's harming.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is hesitation → action that causes injury. The flinch makes sense only before an action with moral weight — typically inflicting harm. The whole paragraph anchors the dilemma in 'pushing someone off a bridge' = harming. So the gerund has to name harming-others.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Encountering' = meeting, coming across. 'Judging' = forming an opinion about. 'Harming' = injuring, causing damage to. 'Disregarding' = ignoring, paying no attention to. Only 'harming' captures the moral weight that justifies a 'flinch' before the act.

steps.title: Plug each option in and test
steps.text: A 'flinch before encountering others' — encountering is just meeting; no moral weight to flinch about. B 'flinch before judging others' — judging is mental; you can do it without action. The paragraph is about ACTION (pushing the man). C 'flinch before harming others' — exactly the moral weight; 'pushing the man' = harming. D 'flinch before disregarding others' — disregarding is ignoring; not strong enough to merit a moral flinch in this context.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. 'Personally harming others' is the deliberate-injury action the trolley dilemma asks about — and the 'flinch' is the moral hesitation that humanises the actor. The other options don't carry enough moral weight to need a flinch.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'personally others' as 'meeting others in person', encountering has the right physical sense.
distractors.why_wrong: Encountering doesn't cause moral hesitation — you don't flinch before meeting people. The whole paragraph frames the dilemma as DOING SOMETHING BAD to someone, not as meeting them. Step 5's plug-and-test shows the mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Judging others' fits a moral paragraph thematically — judging is a moral act, and we do want friends to hesitate before harsh judgements.
distractors.why_wrong: The previous sentences talk about PUSHING SOMEONE OFF A BRIDGE — physical action, not mental judgement. 'Personally' also signals direct action by the friend. Step 2's anchor stays with bodily harm, not opinions.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading: utilitarians 'disregard' rights for outcomes, so 'flinch before disregarding others' might sound like the cure.
distractors.why_wrong: Disregarding is passive (ignoring); the dilemma is active (pushing). And 'flinch before disregarding' is too weak — you wouldn't notice yourself ignoring someone enough to flinch. C names the act with the right weight.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-034.technique: On cloze gerunds in moral-philosophy passages, find the PARADIGM CASE the passage uses (here, pushing a man off a bridge) and identify what kind of action it is (harming someone to help others). The gap usually wants a verb that generalises that paradigm case.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: Moral paragraphs include many adjacent verbs — judging, evaluating, considering — and cloze gerunds invite you to pick a mental verb over a physical one. The cure: track whether the surrounding text is about mental acts or physical ones. Here, 'push' fixes it as physical, and 'harming' is the physical match.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: Gap 35 sits in 'you want your leader to genuinely have – or at least be good at displaying – the right kind of _____'. The closing line clinches it: '...to show that they didn't arrive at it callously'. Leaders should show feelings, not coldness — so the missing noun is 'emotions'. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: Cloze noun — pick what kind of thing a leader needs to 'genuinely have or display' when talking about a hard decision. The clue is the closing phrase 'didn't arrive at it callously'. Callous = unfeeling, cold. The opposite of callous is having feelings. So the gap wants something feelings-related.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The full sentence: 'you want your leader to genuinely have – or at least be good at displaying – the right kind of _____ when they're talking about that decision, to show that they didn't arrive at it callously.' The function of the missing noun is to be the OPPOSITE of callousness. Cold-vs-warm. The noun has to be a feeling word.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Relationship: noun is the antonym of 'callous'. 'Callous' = emotionally cold. Antonym = emotionally warm = having feelings = emotions. Earlier the paragraph said 'we want our friends to at least flinch' — that's an emotional response. The pattern is consistent: the author wants emotion to accompany hard moral choices.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Emotions' = feelings (sadness, regret, hesitation). 'Enthusiasm' = energetic positive feeling, often about something fun. 'Issues' = problems or topics. 'Opinion' = a viewpoint. Only 'emotions' names the cold-vs-warm dimension the sentence is built around.

steps.title: Plug each option in and test
steps.text: A 'the right kind of emotions... not callously' — perfect fit, emotions is the antonym of callous. B 'the right kind of enthusiasm' — enthusiasm doesn't fit hard moral decisions (wars, cuts to benefits); you don't want a leader to be enthusiastic about painful trade-offs. C 'the right kind of issues' — issues are topics, not feelings; doesn't pair with 'callously'. D 'the right kind of opinion' — opinion is a viewpoint, not the absence of callousness; you can have an opinion callously.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The 'callously' anchor at the end of the sentence fixes the meaning: the leader needs to show feelings. 'Emotions' is the only option that names feelings; the others miss the cold-vs-warm dimension entirely.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'the right kind of _____' as 'positive engagement', enthusiasm has the right upbeat ring.
distractors.why_wrong: Enthusiasm is wrong-toned for war declarations or benefit cuts — you don't want a leader excited about painful decisions. The paragraph wants gravitas, which lives in 'emotions' (with shades of regret, hesitation), not enthusiasm. Step 5 catches the tone mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Issues' is a common abstract noun that fits political discourse, and 'right kind of issues' has surface plausibility.
distractors.why_wrong: Issues are topics, not feelings — and the sentence pairs the noun with 'callously', which only makes sense for feelings. Step 3's antonym check shows 'issues' doesn't have a cold-vs-warm dimension.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct in a political-leadership context is 'opinion' — leaders have opinions about decisions.
distractors.why_wrong: Opinions don't connect to 'callously' — you can have a callous opinion. The contrast the sentence builds is callous-vs-feeling, and only 'emotions' lives on the feeling side. Step 5's plug-and-test exposes this.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On cloze nouns where the sentence ends with an antonym-marker ('not callously', 'not coldly', 'never harshly'), the missing noun is usually the antonym of the marker. Read the trailing clause first — it often gives you the answer before you look at the options.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Cloze nouns in political/leadership paragraphs tempt you toward governance-register words ('issues', 'opinion', 'policy'). The cure: anchor on the SPECIFIC contrast the sentence draws. Here, 'callously' fixes the gap as a feeling word — and only 'emotions' fits.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The opening paragraphs say George II's 1728 reform halving mourning periods 'was motivated in large part by a need to assist the textile industry, which often faced ruin when stocks of luxury and coloured fabrics became instantly unsellable'. For many manufacturers, mourning was economically disastrous. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is said about CLOTHING MANUFACTURERS in the FIRST TWO PARAGRAPHS. So you stay in the opening, find the lines that mention manufacturers (or the textile industry), and pick the option that paraphrases those lines.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The decisive line: George II's 1728 reform 'was motivated in large part by a need to assist the textile industry, which often faced ruin when stocks of luxury and coloured fabrics became instantly unsellable'. So when mourning hit, coloured/luxury stock turned instantly worthless and manufacturers risked ruin.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: When a royal died, everyone had to wear black for months — so any manufacturer holding inventory of coloured or luxury fabric was stuck with unsellable goods. The damage was so severe that the king himself shortened mourning periods to protect the industry. For many makers, mourning meant financial disaster.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Stocks' = inventory, goods on hand. 'Unsellable' = unable to be sold. 'Faced ruin' = was on the verge of bankruptcy. 'Halved' = cut in half. The cluster paints a clear picture: mourning was an inventory-destroying event for clothing makers.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says COLOURED FABRICS were difficult to come by — the passage says the opposite: manufacturers HAD stock, but couldn't sell it. B says public mourning was economically disastrous for many manufacturers — exactly the 'faced ruin' line. C says expensive stock was hard to sell AFTER mourning — the passage says it became unsellable DURING mourning (the OPPOSITE timing). D says mourning was generally highly profitable for business — directly contradicted by 'faced ruin'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The 'faced ruin' clause is unambiguous, and the king's intervention to halve mourning periods confirms how serious the damage was for textile manufacturers. B is the exact paraphrase.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to scan 'coloured fabrics' and assume mourning made them scarce — black cloth in demand, colours in short supply.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says coloured fabrics became 'instantly unsellable' — the problem was excess supply, not scarcity. Step 5's match check catches the inversion.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'luxury and coloured fabrics became instantly unsellable' and read it as a post-mourning problem — the leftover stock couldn't move once people returned to colour.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage frames the problem as DURING mourning: stock was unsellable while everyone wore black. After mourning, demand for colour returned. C reverses the timing. Step 3's paraphrase pins down when the damage happened.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on an event everyone participates in (months of black clothing) is to imagine sellers cashing in — surely SOMEONE made money on the demand for mourning clothes.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's later paragraph names ONE winner (Norwich crape makers) but the opening explicitly says the industry as a whole 'faced ruin'. The question asks about the first two paragraphs, where the framing is disaster, not profit.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'what is said about X in the first N paragraphs' items, find the SENTENCE that explicitly names X and let it set the polarity. Here 'textile industry... faced ruin' fixes the polarity as catastrophic; any option that flips this is wrong.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: Long passages mention industry effects in multiple paragraphs with different valences (winners later, losers earlier). The cure: stay strictly inside the paragraph range the question specifies. The full picture comes later; the first two paragraphs only have ruin.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The passage says 'sufficient time was not allowed for the information to reach more provincial regions, let alone Ireland or the north of Scotland, and as a result mourning frequently began late in these areas'. Distant regions got the news late — that's late notification. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about people in MORE DISTANT REGIONS in connection with public mourning. So you find the sentence that talks about provincial / Ireland / Scotland and identify the claim the passage supports about them.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The relevant line: 'Sufficient time was not allowed for the information to reach more provincial regions though, let alone Ireland or the north of Scotland, and as a result mourning frequently began late in these areas.' So distant regions started mourning late because the orders didn't reach them in time.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: London issued mourning instructions with about a week's notice. That worked for southern England but not for places further out — by the time the news reached Ireland or Scotland, the mourning period had already begun. So distant regions were informed late.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Provincial regions' = areas outside the capital, distant from the centre. 'Let alone' = even less so (here: not enough time for provincial areas, and even less for Ireland/Scotland). 'Synchronised' = happening at the same time. The mourning orders aimed for synchronisation, but couldn't achieve it across geography.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says they were more free to choose what to wear — the passage doesn't grant them freedom of choice; the orders applied, they just arrived late. B says they were less interested in participating — the passage doesn't say they were uninterested; the lag was logistical, not motivational. C says they had difficulty finding appropriate clothes — the passage doesn't mention clothing supply problems in distant regions; the issue was timing. D says they got late notification about what clothes were required — exactly the 'information... reach' problem.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage's explicit cause-effect — slow news reaching distant regions → late start to mourning — is what D paraphrases. The other options each invent a different cause (freedom, disinterest, supply) for the late start.

distractors.why_tempting: Later in the passage, Dublin elites do ignore mourning rules — so it's tempting to read distant regions as having more freedom generally.
distractors.why_wrong: The Dublin example comes later and depends on the absence of a local enforcer (the Lord Lieutenant). The question targets the first half of the passage, where distance produces LATE start, not freedom. Step 2's anchor catches the time scope.

distractors.why_tempting: If you imagine people far from London, it's natural to assume they cared less about court funerals — distance breeds indifference.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives a logistical cause (information arrival time), not a motivational one. It says nothing about provincial disinterest. Step 5's option-by-option check shows B imports a cause the text doesn't claim.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap inference: distant regions might have less access to specific mourning fabrics — provincial supply chains weaker than London's.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't mention clothing availability in distant regions. The problem was getting the ORDER (knowing what to wear), not buying the cloth. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the cause precise.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-037.technique: On 'what is implied about X' items where the passage gives a CAUSE-EFFECT chain ('not enough time... as a result...'), match the option that names the CAUSE in the chain, not a different cause. Here the cause is slow-news-reaching-them; D paraphrases that exactly.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: Long passages with later twists (Dublin's defiance) tempt you to import the twist into the first half. The cure: track WHICH paragraph the question is targeting and stay there. The first-half story is logistical (news lag); the later twist (geographical defiance) is a separate point.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The passage tracks mourning from William III's rudimentary order ('deepest mourning that may be') to George I's elaborate phased system with multiple sartorial requirements — and adds that mourning touched churches, courts, letters, even bell-ringing. Public mourning grew more elaborate, involving more dimensions. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks how public mourning CHANGED during the first half of the 18th century. So you look for the contrast the passage draws between early-century and mid-century mourning practices.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two anchors. First: 'In the early part of the century, mourning orders were fairly rudimentary: on the death of William III they stipulated only that all persons put themselves into the deepest mourning that may be.' Second: 'By the time of George I's death, however, mourning had become far more elaborate and the use of phases was emerging, which varied in length and had their own specific sartorial requirements.' Plus: 'mourning touched many other aspects of life' — churches, courts, letters.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Early mourning was a one-line instruction ('wear black, deeply'). By the 1720s, it had become a multi-phase choreography with phase-specific clothes, and it had spread beyond clothing into churches, court events, even letter-writing. So mourning grew in ELABORATENESS and in NUMBER of dimensions it covered.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Rudimentary' = basic, simple, undeveloped. 'Elaborate' = complex, detailed. 'Phases' = stages, distinct periods. 'Sartorial' = related to clothing. 'Pulpit' = the raised platform in church where sermons are given. The vocabulary draws a clear arc from simple → complex.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says prescribed mourning periods grew with each successive monarch — the passage says the OPPOSITE: George II halved them in 1728. B says the public became more negative about participating — the passage doesn't track public attitudes during this period; it tracks practices. C says public mourning started to involve an increasing number of dimensions — exactly the multi-phase + extension-to-churches-courts-letters story. D says people from all walks of life were increasingly forced to participate — the passage doesn't talk about increasing coercion; it talks about elaboration.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The William-III-to-George-I contrast tracks elaboration (more phases) and expansion (more dimensions of life covered). C names both halves of the change.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'Queen Anne, too, was mourned for a year, as was George I' — and you don't notice George II's halving — you might read mourning periods as gradually lengthening.
distractors.why_wrong: George II 'received only six months, thanks to a change he himself had implemented in 1728. This halved all future mourning periods'. So the trend was the OPPOSITE — periods got shorter, not longer. Step 5 catches the direction error.

distractors.why_tempting: The later paragraph mentions 'frequent complaints about the expense and inconvenience' — it's natural to assume public negativity grew over the first half of the century.
distractors.why_wrong: The complaints are mentioned in the broader 18th-century discussion, not specifically tracked across the first half. The question asks about CHANGE during the first half, where the documented change is in PRACTICES, not public attitudes.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on increasingly elaborate state ceremonies is that the state is forcing more people to comply.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes mourning as PERVASIVE ('hard to avoid completely, even for the poor or disinterested') but doesn't claim coercion increased — that pervasiveness was already true. D invents an increase the text doesn't claim.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-038.technique: On 'how did X change during period Y' items, find the EARLY anchor (e.g. William III) and the LATE anchor (e.g. George I), then describe the contrast in a single sentence. Pick the option that names that contrast exactly. Watch for distractors that NAME a real feature of the topic but in the wrong direction.
var-2025-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: Passages that cover a long span tempt you to mix details from different periods (George II's halving, later 18th-century complaints) into the question's target span. The cure: bracket the question's period strictly — first half = early-century-to-1720s — and ignore details outside it.

var-2025-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: The passage says 'George I's passing... had little impact on the theatre industry, as the playhouses were already closed for the summer'. The death essentially had no effect on entertainment because the season was already off. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about consequences for ENTERTAINMENT in relation to GEORGE I's death. So you find the line that mentions theatres or playhouses, and identify the impact described.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The decisive line: 'George I's passing, for instance, had little impact on the theatre industry, as the playhouses were already closed for the summer.' The death happened to fall when entertainment venues were already shut, so the mourning period didn't change their economics meaningfully.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Normally a royal death would force playhouses to close (court functions are suspended, public entertainments are out of step with mourning). But George I died in summer, when theatres were already on their off-season anyway. So the death and the resulting mourning made essentially no difference to the theatre's business.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Little impact' = essentially no effect. 'Already closed' = closed before the death happened. 'For instance' = as an example. The passage uses George I as an example of mourning being LESS DAMAGING than expected, because of timing. So the consequence for entertainment was: essentially none.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says there was a highly POSITIVE effect on entertainment — the passage says little impact, not positive. B says largely NEGATIVE — also wrong; the whole point is that the negative was averted by timing. C says essentially no effects — matches 'had little impact' directly. D says both positive AND negative — the passage doesn't describe two-sided effects; it describes near-zero effect.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. 'Had little impact' translates directly to 'essentially no effects'. The other options invent positive or negative consequences the passage explicitly denies.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'mourning was not all bad' generally and then misattribute the upside to entertainment specifically, A might feel like an inferred positive.
distractors.why_wrong: The general 'not all bad' line refers to OTHER beneficiaries (Norwich crape makers, the rich showing off wealth) — NOT theatre. For entertainment, the passage's specific verdict is 'little impact'. Step 5's option-check catches the misattribution.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'mourning' and 'theatre' and assume the standard negative — royal mourning shuts down public entertainments, theatre suffers.
